news_summary: Scientists have uncovered ancient environmental insights beneath New Zealand and discovered 24 new deep-sea species, while initiatives like Ecosia.org promote sustainability through tree planting, and coordinated efforts are underway to address the oil crisis.
news_summary_fr: Des scientifiques ont mis au jour des informations sur l'environnement d'autrefois sous les fonds marins de la Nouvelle-Zélande et ont découvert 24 nouvelles espèces vivant en eaux profondes, tandis que des initiatives telles qu'Ecosia.org encouragent le développement durable par la plantation d'arbres et que des efforts coordonnés sont en cours pour faire face à la crise pétrolière.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han desvelado datos sobre el medio ambiente en la antigüedad bajo el territorio de Nueva Zelanda y han descubierto 24 nuevas especies de las profundidades marinas, mientras que iniciativas como Ecosia.org promueven la sostenibilidad mediante la plantación de árboles y se están llevando a cabo esfuerzos coordinados para hacer frente a la crisis del petróleo.

articles.title: Scientists open a million-year-old time capsule beneath New Zealand
articles.summary: Deep inside a cave, scientists uncovered fossils from 16 species, including a newfound kākāpō ancestor that may have been able to fly. These remains reveal that New Zealand’s ecosystems were constantly disrupted by volcanic eruptions and rapid climate shifts. Long before humans, waves of extinction and replacement reshaped the islands’ wildlife. It’s a rare window into a missing chapter of natural history.
articles.content:
Scientists from Australia and New Zealand have uncovered ancient animal remains inside a cave near Waitomo on Aotearoa's North Island. The site contains a large collection of fossils dating back about 1 million years, including a previously unknown ancestor of the kākāpō, a large flightless parrot.

The discovery includes fossils from 12 species of birds and four species of frogs, offering a rare glimpse into what New Zealand's ecosystems looked like during that time.

Climate Change and Volcanoes Drove Ancient Extinctions

The findings show that New Zealand's wildlife was heavily shaped by dramatic climate shifts and powerful volcanic eruptions. These natural events led to repeated waves of extinction and the emergence of new species long before humans arrived, according to research published in Alcheringa: An Australasian Journal of Palaeontology.

Lead author, Flinders University Associate Professor Trevor Worthy, says the research reveals a previously unknown chapter of the country's biodiversity.

"This is a newly recognized avifauna for New Zealand, one that was replaced by the one humans encountered a million years later," says Associate Professor Worthy, from the College of Science and Engineering at Flinders University.

"This remarkable find suggests our ancient forests were once home to a diverse group of birds that did not survive the next million years."

The fossils were examined by paleontologists from Flinders University and Canterbury Museum, working alongside volcanologists Joel Baker from the University of Auckland and Simon Barker of Victoria University of Wellington.

The team estimates that roughly 33-50% of species disappeared in the million years leading up to human arrival in Aotearoa New Zealand.

Co-author and Canterbury Museum Senior Curator of Natural History Dr. Paul Scofield explains that rapid environmental changes were a major factor.

"These extinctions were driven by relatively rapid climate shifts and cataclysmic volcanic eruptions," says Dr. Scofield.

"From our excavations at St Bathans in Central Otago over many years, we have a snapshot of life in Aotearoa between 20 and 16 million years ago. These new findings cast light on the 15 million year period from then to 1 million years ago, which is largely absent from New Zealand's fossil record," says Dr. Scofield.

"This wasn't a missing chapter in New Zealand's ancient history, it was a missing volume."

Ancient Kākāpō Relative May Have Been Able to Fly

Among the most notable discoveries is a newly identified parrot species, Strigops insulaborealis, an ancient relative of the modern kākāpō. While today's kākāpō is known for being heavy and flightless, this earlier species may have had the ability to fly.

Fossil analysis suggests it had weaker legs than the modern bird, indicating it was likely less suited for climbing. Researchers say further study is needed to confirm whether it could truly fly.

The cave also contained fossils of an extinct ancestor of the takahē, helping scientists better understand the evolution of this well-known New Zealand bird. In addition, researchers found an extinct pigeon species closely related to Australian bronzewing pigeons.

"The shifting forest and shrubland habitats forced a reset of the bird populations," adds Dr. Scofield.

"We believe this was a major driver for the evolutionary diversification of birds and other fauna in the North Island."

Volcanic Ash Helps Date the Fossils

Scientists were able to determine the age of the fossils because they were preserved between two distinct layers of volcanic ash inside the cave. One layer dates to an eruption about 1.55 million years ago, while the other comes from a massive eruption around 1 million years ago.

The later eruption likely covered much of the North Island in meters of ash. Although much of that material was eventually washed away, some remained protected within caves. The presence of the older ash layer also indicates that this site is the oldest known cave in the North Island.

A Missing Piece of New Zealand's Natural History

Associate Professor Worthy says the discovery fills a major gap in understanding the country's past.

The fossils "provide a critical, missing baseline for New Zealand's natural history."

"For decades, the extinction of New Zealand's birds was viewed primarily through the lens of human arrival 750 years ago. This study proves that natural forces like super-volcanoes and dramatic climate shifts were already sculpting the unique identity of our wildlife over a million years ago."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of million-year-old fossils in New Zealand that reveals how natural forces shaped the country's biodiversity long before humans arrived. This discovery fills a major gap in understanding New Zealand's natural history and evolutionary processes, providing broad insights into climate impact and species evolution with meaningful environmental and scientific implications.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover million-year-old fossils revealing New Zealand's ancient wildlife changes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied New Zealand's unique animals and plants, but there was a large gap in knowledge about what lived there before humans arrived 750 years ago. Fossils from millions of years ago were rare, making it hard to understand how natural events shaped the islands’ wildlife.

**What happened** – A team of scientists from Australia and New Zealand found fossils inside a cave near Waitomo on New Zealand’s North Island. These fossils date back about 1 million years and include remains from 16 species, such as birds and frogs. Among the discoveries is a new ancient relative of the kākāpō, a flightless parrot, which may have been able to fly. The fossils were preserved between layers of volcanic ash, helping scientists know their age.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that New Zealand’s ecosystems were shaped by volcanic eruptions and quick climate changes long before humans arrived. These events caused many species to disappear and new ones to appear. The fossils fill an important missing chapter in New Zealand’s natural history, revealing how wildlife changed over time and helping scientists understand how animals like the kākāpō evolved.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to study these fossils more closely to learn if the ancient kākāpō could really fly and to better understand how New Zealand’s animals adapted to changing environments. This work could lead to new discoveries about other species and how nature responds to big changes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists uncovered million-year-old fossils in New Zealand that reveal how volcanic eruptions and climate shifts shaped the islands’ wildlife long before humans arrived.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des fossiles vieux d’un million d’années révélant les changements anciens de la faune en Nouvelle-Zélande
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps les animaux et plantes uniques de la Nouvelle-Zélande, mais il manquait des informations sur ce qui vivait là avant l’arrivée des humains il y a 750 ans. Les fossiles très anciens étaient rares, rendant difficile la compréhension de l’évolution de la faune de l’île.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de scientifiques d’Australie et de Nouvelle-Zélande a découvert des fossiles dans une grotte près de Waitomo, sur l’île du Nord. Ces fossiles datent d’environ un million d’années et incluent 16 espèces, comme des oiseaux et des grenouilles. Parmi ces fossiles, un ancêtre inconnu du kākāpō, un perroquet incapable de voler, qui aurait peut-être pu voler. Les fossiles ont été protégés entre des couches de cendres volcaniques, ce qui a permis de dater leur âge.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les écosystèmes de Nouvelle-Zélande ont été façonnés par des éruptions volcaniques et des changements climatiques rapides bien avant l’arrivée des humains. Ces événements ont causé la disparition de nombreuses espèces et l’apparition de nouvelles. Ces fossiles comblent un chapitre important manquant de l’histoire naturelle du pays, aidant à comprendre l’évolution d’animaux comme le kākāpō.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier ces fossiles plus en détail pour savoir si l’ancien kākāpō pouvait vraiment voler et mieux comprendre comment les animaux se sont adaptés aux changements de leur environnement. Ce travail pourrait permettre de nouvelles découvertes sur d’autres espèces et sur la réaction de la nature aux grands changements.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des fossiles vieux d’un million d’années découverts en Nouvelle-Zélande révèlent comment les éruptions volcaniques et les changements climatiques ont façonné la faune avant l’arrivée des humains.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren fósiles de un millón de años que revelan antiguos cambios en la fauna de Nueva Zelanda
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo los animales y plantas únicos de Nueva Zelanda, pero había poca información sobre lo que vivía allí antes de la llegada de los humanos hace 750 años. Los fósiles muy antiguos eran escasos, dificultando entender cómo cambió la fauna de las islas.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de científicos de Australia y Nueva Zelanda encontró fósiles dentro de una cueva cerca de Waitomo, en la Isla Norte. Estos fósiles tienen aproximadamente un millón de años e incluyen restos de 16 especies, como aves y ranas. Entre los hallazgos está un nuevo pariente antiguo del kākāpō, un loro incapaz de volar, que tal vez sí podía volar. Los fósiles estaban entre capas de ceniza volcánica, lo que ayudó a determinar su edad.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que los ecosistemas de Nueva Zelanda fueron moldeados por erupciones volcánicas y cambios climáticos rápidos mucho antes de la llegada de los humanos. Estos eventos causaron la extinción de muchas especies y la aparición de otras nuevas. Los fósiles llenan un capítulo importante que faltaba en la historia natural del país y ayudan a entender cómo evolucionaron animales como el kākāpō.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar estos fósiles con más detalle para saber si el antiguo kākāpō realmente podía volar y para entender mejor cómo los animales se adaptaron a los cambios ambientales. Este trabajo podría llevar a nuevos descubrimientos sobre otras especies y la respuesta de la naturaleza a grandes cambios.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos descubrieron fósiles de un millón de años en Nueva Zelanda que muestran cómo las erupciones volcánicas y cambios climáticos moldearon la fauna antes de la llegada humana.
articles.image_prompt: An ancient cave interior softly illuminated by warm golden light filtering through cracks, showcasing detailed fossils of a large flightless parrot ancestor and extinct birds embedded in layers of volcanic ash, with subtle silhouettes of erupting volcanoes and shifting forest landscapes visible through a distant cave opening.

articles.title: 24 new deep-sea species found including a rare new branch of life
articles.summary: In a remarkable deep-sea breakthrough, researchers have discovered 24 new species of amphipods in the Pacific’s Clarion-Clipperton Zone—including a rare, entirely new superfamily. The findings reveal previously unknown branches of life and push the boundaries of how deep these creatures are known to live.
articles.content:
Scientists have identified 24 previously unknown deep-sea amphipod species -- including one entirely new superfamily -- in the Clarion-Clipperton Zone (CCZ) in the central Pacific Ocean.

The findings, published March 24 in an open-access ZooKeys special issue, represent a major step forward in documenting life in the CCZ. This vast region stretches across six million square kilometers between Hawai'i and Mexico and remains one of the least understood ecosystems on Earth.

The project was led by Dr. Anna Jażdżewska of the University of Lodz (UL) and Tammy Horton of the National Oceanography Centre (NOC). A team of 16 specialists and early-career researchers gathered for a week-long taxonomy workshop in 2024 at UL's Department of Invertebrate Zoology and Hydrobiology, where they focused on identifying and describing amphipod species from the region.

This work contributes to the International Seabed Authority's Sustainable Seabed Knowledge Initiative (SSKI) and its 'One Thousand Reasons' project, which aims to formally describe 1,000 new species by the end of the decade.

Rare Evolutionary Discoveries and New Species Groups

The study revealed several major scientific milestones. In total, the team described 24 new species across 10 amphipod families, including both predators and scavengers.

Among the most significant findings were:

A newly identified family (Mirabestiidae) and superfamily (Mirabestioidea), representing entirely new branches on the evolutionary tree.

Two new genera (Mirabestia and Pseudolepechinella).

The deepest recorded occurrences for several known genera.

The first molecular barcodes for a number of rare species.

Dr. Tammy Horton said: "To find a new superfamily is incredibly exciting, and very rarely happens so this is a discovery we will all remember.

"With more than 90% of species in the CCZ still unnamed, each species described is a vital step towards improving our understanding of this fascinating ecosystem.

"Describing the species encountered during these studies is a critical step in documenting the rich biodiversity of the CCZ, enabling us to communicate effectively about the fauna."

Global Collaboration Advances Deep-Sea Biodiversity Research

Careful classification, known as taxonomy, is essential for understanding the animals that live in the CCZ. It provides key information about species identity, distribution, and ecological roles within this delicate environment.

Researchers from the National Oceanography Centre described eight of the new species, working alongside collaborators from institutions around the world. These included the University of Lodz, Natural History Museum in London, Canadian Museum of Nature, Earth Sciences New Zealand (NIWA), University of Hamburg, Senckenberg -- Leibniz Institution for Biodiversity and Earth System Research (SGN), and the University Museum of Bergen.

The project also demonstrated how effective coordinated taxonomy workshops can be, offering a faster and more collaborative approach to species discovery.

Anna Jażdżewska, University of Lodz, said: "This was a truly collaborative process that allowed us to achieve the ambitious goal of describing more than 20 species new to science within a year -- something that would not have been possible if each of us worked independently.

"The team's findings provide information that is crucial for future conservation and policy decisions, and it highlights how important it is for this work to continue."

At the current pace of roughly 25 new species described each year, scientists estimate that amphipods in the eastern CCZ could be nearly fully cataloged within the next decade.

How New Deep-Sea Species Get Their Names

Each newly discovered species must be given a scientific name, and researchers often draw inspiration from people, experiences, or even popular culture.

Many of the 24 species received names that reflect personal connections. Co-leads Dr. Tammy Horton and Anna Jażdżewska were both honored with species named after them: Byblis hortonae, Thrombasia ania, and Byblisoides jazdzewskae (respectively).

Dr. Horton also named a species in the new superfamily (Mirabestia maisie) after her daughter, who had long waited to join her siblings in receiving that distinction.

The team also recognized the World Register of Marine Species (WoRMS) by naming Eperopeus vermiculatus in its honor, calling WoRMS a 'wonderful resource for all marine taxonomists'.

Early-career scientists (including students) contributed to the naming process as well, often honoring family members or drawing creative connections between deep-sea life and digital worlds. One species, Lepidepecreum myla, was named for a video game character, with the author noting that both 'are just little arthropods trying to survive in total darkness.'

Language also played a role in naming. The species Pseudolepechinella apricity reflects a sense of warmth and friendship that developed during the workshop.

Dr. Horton revealed: "Apricity means the feeling of the warmth of the winter sun, and it is one of my favorite words. It was very apt to use during the workshop as we discussed our findings in the warmth of the February sun amid the snow of the Polish winter in Lodz. It was certainly fitting to also use it for one of our amphipod discoveries.

"We came together as research colleagues, but the spirit of collaboration and shared experience shone through, so it was important to recognize that in our work."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of 24 new deep-sea species, including a rare new superfamily, expanding knowledge of biodiversity in a vast and little-understood ocean region. This discovery has broad implications for marine biology, conservation, and future policy decisions, representing a meaningful advancement in environmental science and taxonomy.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover 24 new deep-sea species including a rare new branch of life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Clarion-Clipperton Zone (CCZ) is a huge area in the central Pacific Ocean between Hawai'i and Mexico. It covers six million square kilometers and is one of the least explored places on Earth. Scientists want to learn more about the life that lives deep under the ocean here.

**What happened** – A group of researchers found 24 new species of tiny deep-sea animals called amphipods in the CCZ. These creatures are small crustaceans that live on the ocean floor. Among these discoveries, they identified a completely new superfamily, which is a big new group in the tree of life. The team worked together during a week-long workshop in Poland in 2024 and published their findings in March.

**Impact** – Finding a new superfamily is very rare and shows how much we still don’t know about life in the ocean’s depths. These discoveries help scientists understand the variety of life in the CCZ and how these animals live. Knowing this is important for protecting the ocean and making smart decisions about how to use the seabed. The project also showed how working together across countries can speed up scientific discoveries.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to keep studying and naming new species in the CCZ. At the current rate, they think they can identify almost all amphipods in the area within ten years. This work will help guide conservation efforts and policies to protect deep-sea ecosystems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By discovering 24 new deep-sea species, including a rare new superfamily, scientists are opening a new chapter in understanding life in one of Earth's least explored ocean regions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent 24 nouvelles espèces des grands fonds, dont une nouvelle branche rare de la vie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La zone Clarion-Clipperton (CCZ) est une vaste région du centre de l'océan Pacifique, entre Hawaï et le Mexique. Elle couvre six millions de kilomètres carrés et reste l'un des endroits les moins explorés de la planète. Les scientifiques cherchent à mieux comprendre la vie qui habite les profondeurs de cet océan.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un groupe de chercheurs a découvert 24 nouvelles espèces de petits animaux des grands fonds appelés amphipodes dans la CCZ. Ces créatures sont de petits crustacés qui vivent au fond de l'océan. Parmi ces découvertes, ils ont identifié une toute nouvelle superfamille, un grand groupe nouveau dans l’arbre de la vie. L'équipe a travaillé ensemble lors d’un atelier d’une semaine en Pologne en 2024 et a publié ses résultats en mars.

**Impact** – Trouver une nouvelle superfamille est très rare et montre à quel point nous connaissons encore peu la vie dans les profondeurs océaniques. Ces découvertes aident les scientifiques à mieux comprendre la diversité de la vie dans la CCZ et comment ces animaux vivent. Ces connaissances sont importantes pour protéger l’océan et prendre des décisions éclairées sur l’utilisation du fond marin. Le projet a aussi montré que la collaboration internationale accélère les découvertes scientifiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de continuer à étudier et nommer de nouvelles espèces dans la CCZ. Au rythme actuel, ils pensent pouvoir identifier presque tous les amphipodes de la région d’ici dix ans. Ce travail aidera à guider la conservation et les politiques pour protéger les écosystèmes des grands fonds.

**En une phrase** – En découvrant 24 nouvelles espèces des grands fonds, dont une rare nouvelle superfamille, les scientifiques ouvrent un nouveau chapitre dans la compréhension de la vie dans l’une des régions océaniques les moins explorées de la Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren 24 nuevas especies de aguas profundas, incluyendo una nueva rama rara de la vida
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Zona Clarion-Clipperton (CCZ) es una gran área en el centro del Océano Pacífico, entre Hawái y México. Cubre seis millones de kilómetros cuadrados y es uno de los lugares menos explorados del planeta. Los científicos quieren aprender más sobre la vida que existe en las profundidades del océano allí.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo de investigadores descubrió 24 nuevas especies de pequeños animales de aguas profundas llamados anfípodos en la CCZ. Estos animales son pequeños crustáceos que viven en el fondo del océano. Entre estos hallazgos, identificaron una superfamilia completamente nueva, que es un grupo grande y raro en el árbol de la vida. El equipo trabajó junto durante un taller de una semana en Polonia en 2024 y publicó sus resultados en marzo.

**Impacto** – Encontrar una nueva superfamilia es muy raro y muestra cuánto desconocemos sobre la vida en las profundidades del océano. Estos descubrimientos ayudan a los científicos a entender la variedad de vida en la CCZ y cómo viven estos animales. Conocer esto es importante para proteger el océano y tomar decisiones inteligentes sobre el uso del lecho marino. El proyecto también mostró que trabajar en equipo entre países puede acelerar los descubrimientos científicos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean seguir estudiando y nombrando nuevas especies en la CCZ. Al ritmo actual, creen que podrían identificar casi todos los anfípodos del área en diez años. Este trabajo ayudará a guiar los esfuerzos de conservación y las políticas para proteger los ecosistemas de aguas profundas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Al descubrir 24 nuevas especies de aguas profundas, incluyendo una rara nueva superfamilia, los científicos abren un nuevo capítulo para entender la vida en una de las regiones oceánicas menos exploradas de la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a deep ocean scene showing a diverse array of intricately drawn, uniquely shaped amphipod-like creatures glowing softly in the dark waters of the Clarion-Clipperton Zone, surrounded by symbolic branching evolutionary trees made of delicate coral-like structures, with subtle hints of collaborative human presence represented by intertwined, glowing threads connecting the creatures, evoking discovery and global scientific teamwork.

articles.title: Classic Sustainability In Your Ear: Ecosia.org’s Christian Kroll on Planting Trees With Every Web Search
articles.summary:
How do you reduce your digital life’s environmental impact? Making changes to reduce your environmental...
The post Classic Sustainability In Your Ear: Ecosia.org’s Christian Kroll on Planting Trees With Every Web Search appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
How do you reduce your digital life’s environmental impact? Making changes to reduce your environmental impact around the house is straightforward — you can eat less meat, reduce your purchases of single-use plastic or turn down the thermostat by a few degrees to make a difference. But when you go online, there aren’t many obvious choices to cut your impact. Enter Ecosia.org, which has planted more than 143 million trees to offset the environmental impact of web searches. Ecosia remains a stalwart of ecologically responsible tech four years after this interview.

Christian started Ecosia in 2009 after seeing the devastating impact of deforestation first-hand while traveling after graduating from college. The company was also the first B Corporation in Germany. While the search engine does produce CO2, the trees planted offset more emissions than ecosia.org creates — they estimate that the trees planted result in a net reduction of CO2 of 2.2 lbs. per search. To put that in context, Ecosia estimates that if it had the same volume of searches as Google, it could plant enough trees to remove 15% of humanity’s CO2 emissions each year. You can search, plant trees, and learn more at ecosia.org.

Subscribe to Sustainability in Your Ear on iTunes and Apple Podcasts.

Follow Sustainability in Your Ear on Spreaker, iHeartRadio, or YouTube

Editor’s Note: This episode originally aired on February 16. 2022.
articles.reasoning: The article describes Ecosia.org's impactful environmental initiative of planting trees to offset CO2 emissions from web searches, demonstrating a meaningful and scalable positive effect on climate action with clear context and measurable benefits.
articles.personality_title: Ecosia.org plants trees with every web search to fight climate change
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people want to reduce their impact on the environment, like using less plastic or saving energy at home. But it is harder to know how to be green when using the internet.

**What happened** – Ecosia.org, a search engine started in 2009 by Christian Kroll, plants trees every time someone uses it to search the web. The company has planted over 143 million trees so far. Even though using the internet creates some carbon emissions, Ecosia’s trees absorb more CO2 than the searches produce.

**Impact** – Ecosia estimates that for every search, their trees remove about 2.2 pounds of CO2. If Ecosia had as many users as Google, it could remove 15% of the world’s carbon emissions each year. This shows how small actions online can add up to big environmental benefits.

**What's next step** – Ecosia will keep planting trees and encouraging people to switch to their search engine. More users could help increase the positive effect on the planet.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using Ecosia.org for web searches helps plant trees that reduce more carbon dioxide than the searches create, making it a simple way to support the environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Ecosia.org plante des arbres à chaque recherche pour lutter contre le changement climatique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de gens veulent réduire leur impact sur l’environnement, par exemple en utilisant moins de plastique ou en économisant de l’énergie à la maison. Mais il est plus difficile de savoir comment agir écologiquement sur Internet.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Ecosia.org, un moteur de recherche créé en 2009 par Christian Kroll, plante des arbres à chaque fois qu’une personne l’utilise. L’entreprise a déjà planté plus de 143 millions d’arbres. Même si utiliser Internet produit un peu de CO2, les arbres d’Ecosia en absorbent plus que ce que les recherches génèrent.

**Impact** – Ecosia estime que chaque recherche permet d’éliminer environ 1 kg de CO2 grâce aux arbres plantés. Si Ecosia avait autant d’utilisateurs que Google, elle pourrait retirer 15 % des émissions mondiales de carbone chaque année. Cela montre que de petits gestes en ligne peuvent avoir un grand effet pour la planète.

**Étape suivante** – Ecosia continuera à planter des arbres et à encourager les gens à utiliser leur moteur de recherche. Plus d’utilisateurs pourraient augmenter cet impact positif.

**Une phrase clé** – Utiliser Ecosia.org pour ses recherches sur Internet aide à planter des arbres qui éliminent plus de dioxyde de carbone que les recherches n’en produisent, une façon simple de soutenir l’environnement.
articles.personality_title_es: Ecosia.org planta árboles con cada búsqueda para combatir el cambio climático
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas quieren reducir su impacto ambiental, como usar menos plástico o ahorrar energía en casa. Pero es más difícil saber cómo ser ecológico cuando se usa Internet.

**Qué pasó** – Ecosia.org, un motor de búsqueda creado en 2009 por Christian Kroll, planta árboles cada vez que alguien lo usa para buscar en la web. La empresa ha plantado más de 143 millones de árboles hasta ahora. Aunque usar Internet produce algo de CO2, los árboles de Ecosia absorben más dióxido de carbono del que generan las búsquedas.

**Impacto** – Ecosia calcula que por cada búsqueda, sus árboles eliminan alrededor de 1 kilo de CO2. Si Ecosia tuviera tantos usuarios como Google, podría eliminar el 15 % de las emisiones mundiales de carbono cada año. Esto muestra cómo pequeñas acciones en línea pueden sumar grandes beneficios para el medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – Ecosia seguirá plantando árboles y animando a más personas a usar su buscador. Más usuarios podrían aumentar el efecto positivo en el planeta.

**Una frase para llevarse** – Usar Ecosia.org para buscar en internet ayuda a plantar árboles que eliminan más dióxido de carbono del que generan las búsquedas, una forma sencilla de apoyar al medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing vintage computer keyboard with green leaves and small saplings sprouting gently from the keys, connected by delicate roots that intertwine into a flourishing forest growing upward, symbolizing web searches directly nurturing tree growth and environmental renewal.

articles.title: What governments and households are being asked to do in the oil crisis
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM The International Energy Agency has already done something it has never done before: ordered the largest release of government oil reserves in its history. Now it is turning to the demand side… and asking a lot of people to make some small changes quickly. Why the Iran disruption is […]
The post What governments and households are being asked to do in the oil crisis first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

The International Energy Agency has already done something it has never done before: ordered the largest release of government oil reserves in its history. Now it is turning to the demand side… and asking a lot of people to make some small changes quickly.

Why the Iran disruption is different this time

Military strikes on Iran have triggered the most significant supply disruption in the history of the global oil market. The Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway through which a large share of the world’s oil passes and which Iran largely controls, is at the center of the crisis. Prices for petrol, diesel, jet fuel, and LPG have all risen sharply in response.

The IEA, which advises member countries including the US, the UK, and Australia, has warned that without a swift resolution, “the impacts on energy markets and economies are set to become more and more severe.” The emergency reserve release helped. It will not be enough on its own.

What the IEA’s 10 demand measures actually include

Most of the recommendations focus on road transport, which accounts for a large share of daily oil consumption. At the top of the list: work from home where possible. The IEA also recommends reducing highway speed limits by at least 10 kilometers per hour (about 6 miles per hour), a change that has a measurable effect on fuel economy at scale, and expanding public transit use and car sharing.

Cities are addressed specifically. The IEA recommends number-plate rotation schemes in large urban areas, where vehicles with odd and even plate numbers alternate road access on different weekdays, to reduce overall traffic volume.

Commercial transport gets its own set of recommendations: better load management for trucks, improved vehicle maintenance, and switching between petrochemical feedstocks to free up LPG. For households, the IEA suggests electric cooking where possible and avoiding air travel when alternatives exist.

How governments are already responding to the oil crisis

Several countries have not waited. Austria and Greece have capped profit margins at fuel retailers, keeping pump prices from compounding the supply shock. In the UK, vulnerable households are receiving financial help with heating oil costs. Multiple countries have restricted non-essential travel for public officials and launched national campaigns encouraging people and businesses to reduce consumption.

The IEA made clear that demand-side tools are not optional for member nations. They are obligations. “Demand restraint is one of the emergency response measures that all IEA member countries are required to have ready as a contingency,” the agency said, “and that they can use to contribute to an IEA collective action in the event of an emergency like this current disruption.”

IEA executive director Fatih Birol described the 10-point list as a “menu of immediate and concrete measures that can be taken on the demand side by governments, businesses and households to shelter consumers from the impacts of this crisis.”

What households and businesses can do right now

None of the recommended actions require new infrastructure. Most can be adopted this week: an updated speed limit, a remote work policy, a carpool. The IEA’s case, essentially, is that the response to a global oil shock does not have to start with a sweeping government intervention. It can start with where you work and how fast you drive. The scale comes from everyone doing it at once.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant, coordinated global response to an unprecedented oil supply crisis, focusing on demand-side measures that can reduce consumption quickly and broadly. These measures have tangible, large-scale benefits for energy security and environmental impact by encouraging fuel savings and reduced emissions. The story is substantive, detailing specific actions governments, businesses, and households can take immediately, and highlights meaningful collective action with broad societal relevance.
articles.personality_title: Global call for simple actions to ease oil crisis
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
A sudden military conflict near Iran has disrupted much of the world’s oil supply. This has caused prices for petrol, diesel, and other fuels to rise sharply. The International Energy Agency (IEA), which advises many countries, has taken emergency steps to release oil reserves but says more is needed.

**What happened**
The IEA has asked governments, businesses, and households to make quick changes to reduce oil use. They suggested ten simple actions like working from home, driving slower by about 6 miles per hour, using public transport, and sharing cars. Some countries have already started helping by limiting fuel prices and supporting vulnerable people with heating costs.

**Impact**
This approach is unique because it focuses on many small changes by lots of people instead of just relying on governments to act alone. These actions can quickly reduce fuel use without needing new technology or big investments. For example, lowering speed limits saves fuel across millions of vehicles every day.

**What's next step**
Countries will continue to encourage these demand-side measures while monitoring the oil supply situation. If more problems arise, the IEA may ask for stronger actions. Meanwhile, households and businesses can start making these changes immediately to help ease the crisis.

**One-sentence takeaway**
Simple changes like driving slower and working from home can add up worldwide to reduce oil use during a major supply crisis.
articles.personality_title_fr: Appel mondial à des actions simples pour atténuer la crise pétrolière
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Un conflit militaire soudain près de l’Iran a perturbé une grande partie de l’approvisionnement mondial en pétrole. Cela a fait grimper fortement les prix de l’essence, du diesel et d’autres carburants. L’Agence internationale de l’énergie (AIE), qui conseille de nombreux pays, a pris des mesures d’urgence en libérant des réserves de pétrole, mais affirme que cela ne suffit pas.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
L’AIE a demandé aux gouvernements, entreprises et ménages de faire rapidement des changements pour réduire leur consommation de pétrole. Elle a proposé dix actions simples comme travailler à domicile, rouler plus lentement d’environ 10 km/h, utiliser les transports en commun et partager les voitures. Certains pays ont déjà commencé à limiter les prix du carburant et à aider les personnes vulnérables pour leurs coûts de chauffage.

**Impact**
Cette approche est unique car elle mise sur de nombreux petits gestes de la part de beaucoup de personnes, plutôt que de compter uniquement sur les gouvernements. Ces actions peuvent rapidement réduire la consommation de carburant sans nécessiter de nouvelles technologies ou de gros investissements. Par exemple, réduire la vitesse limite fait économiser du carburant à des millions de véhicules chaque jour.

**Prochaine étape**
Les pays continueront à encourager ces mesures côté demande tout en surveillant la situation de l’approvisionnement en pétrole. Si les problèmes persistent, l’AIE pourrait demander des actions plus fortes. En attendant, les ménages et entreprises peuvent commencer à appliquer ces changements immédiatement pour aider à calmer la crise.

**Une phrase clé**
Des changements simples comme rouler plus lentement et travailler à domicile peuvent réduire ensemble la consommation de pétrole lors d’une crise majeure d’approvisionnement.
articles.personality_title_es: Llamado global a acciones simples para aliviar la crisis del petróleo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Un conflicto militar cerca de Irán ha interrumpido gran parte del suministro mundial de petróleo. Esto ha provocado un fuerte aumento en los precios de la gasolina, el diésel y otros combustibles. La Agencia Internacional de la Energía (AIE), que asesora a muchos países, ha tomado medidas de emergencia liberando reservas de petróleo, pero dice que no es suficiente.

**Qué pasó**
La AIE ha pedido a gobiernos, empresas y hogares que hagan cambios rápidos para reducir el uso de petróleo. Sugirió diez acciones simples como trabajar desde casa, reducir la velocidad en unos 10 kilómetros por hora, usar transporte público y compartir autos. Algunos países ya han comenzado a limitar los precios del combustible y a apoyar a personas vulnerables con los costos de calefacción.

**Impacto**
Este enfoque es único porque se basa en muchos pequeños cambios realizados por muchas personas, en lugar de depender solo de los gobiernos. Estas acciones pueden reducir rápidamente el consumo de combustible sin necesitar nuevas tecnologías o grandes inversiones. Por ejemplo, bajar los límites de velocidad ahorra combustible en millones de vehículos cada día.

**Próximo paso**
Los países seguirán promoviendo estas medidas del lado de la demanda mientras vigilan la situación del suministro de petróleo. Si surgen más problemas, la AIE podría pedir acciones más fuertes. Mientras tanto, hogares y empresas pueden comenzar a hacer estos cambios de inmediato para ayudar a aliviar la crisis.

**Una frase clave**
Cambios simples como manejar más despacio y trabajar desde casa pueden sumar en todo el mundo para reducir el uso de petróleo durante una crisis grave de suministro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a calm cityscape with softly glowing streetlights and empty roads featuring a few stylized car silhouettes spaced apart, a large symbolic oil drop gently cradled by two hands above the city, and subtle icons of a house with a Wi-Fi signal and a bus filled with abstract passengers, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones.
